metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/reducer/_managed_refs.py` to the Files list with an explicit note that `caused_by` must remain **absent** from `MANAGED_REF_KINDS` (the only explicit unmapped-relation set), and remove the ambiguous 'add it to the unmapped set if that set is explicit' instruction for `outbound_links.py`/`acli_graph.py`, which use implicit absence (not an explicit set).
evidence: outbound_links.py _RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK = {'blocks': ..., 'depends_on': ..., 'relates_to': ...} — uses implicit absence, no explicit unmapped set
acli_graph.py _RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK = same three-entry dict — same implicit-absence pattern

src/rebar/reducer/_managed_refs.py line 50: MANAGED_REF_KINDS: tuple[str, ...] = ('parent', 'blocks', 'depends_on', 'relates_to') — THIS is the only explicit named set; caused_by must remain absent from it (correct) but the file is absent from the plan's Files list
_managed_refs.py is entirely absent from the plan's Files list
findings.finding: The plan instructs adding caused_by to 'the unmapped set if that set is explicit' in outbound_links.py and acli_graph.py, but those files use implicit absence (absent dict key = skipped, no explicit unmapped set), while the only real explicit named set is MANAGED_REF_KINDS in src/rebar/reducer/_managed_refs.py — a file entirely absent from the plan's Files list. An implementer following the plan's instruction in the wrong files will either make a no-op edit or become confused; the _managed_refs.py file (where caused_by must correctly remain absent) goes unverified.
findings.location: Files section: 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/acli_graph.py, outbound_links.py — leave caused_by intentionally Jira-unmapped ... add it to the unmapped set if that set is explicit'
scenarios: Implementer searches outbound_links.py and acli_graph.py for an 'unmapped set' to update, finds none, is confused about where to act

Implementer misses verifying _managed_refs.py and accidentally adds caused_by to MANAGED_REF_KINDS, causing it to be treated as a Jira-managed relation and triggering spurious link-removal propagation to Jira
findings.suggested_fix: In the Files section, replace the current outbound_links.py/acli_graph.py note with: 'outbound_links.py, acli_graph.py — no change needed; both use implicit absence via _RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK (absent key = skipped). Also verify src/rebar/reducer/_managed_refs.py: caused_by must remain absent from MANAGED_REF_KINDS (which lists Jira-synced relations); adding it there would trigger spurious Jira link-removal propagation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC for documentation: e.g. 'Each of `docs/ticket-model.md`, `docs/event-schema.md`, and `docs/cli-reference.md` mentions `caused_by` — verified by `grep -l caused_by docs/ticket-model.md docs/event-schema.md docs/cli-reference.md` returning all three paths.'
evidence: Three doc files are listed under Files with 'document the relation' as their goal.
None of the five ACs reference the docs — there is no proving command, grep, or CI check that would catch missing/incorrect documentation.
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion covers the documentation updates listed in the Files section (`docs/ticket-model.md`, `docs/event-schema.md`, `docs/cli-reference.md`), leaving those deliverables unverifiable and creating a gap between 'what we'll do' and 'what done looks like'.
findings.location: Files section — docs/*.md bullets; Acceptance Criteria section
scenarios: An executor ships all code changes with passing tests but omits the doc updates; the plan's ACs all pass green and the work is signed off, leaving the docs stale.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: `grep -rn 'caused_by' docs/ticket-model.md docs/event-schema.md docs/cli-reference.md | wc -l` returns ≥ 3 (one mention each).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion covering the three documentation files (e.g. 'docs/ticket-model.md, docs/event-schema.md, and docs/cli-reference.md each enumerate `caused_by` in their relation lists').
evidence: Files section lists 'docs/ticket-model.md, docs/event-schema.md, docs/cli-reference.md — document the relation'
None of AC1–AC5 references documentation correctness or completeness
findings.finding: Three documentation deliverables (`docs/ticket-model.md`, `docs/event-schema.md`, `docs/cli-reference.md`) are listed in the Files section but no acceptance criterion covers them — they are orphaned deliverables with no verifiable pass/fail gate.
findings.location: Files section — `docs/ticket-model.md`, `docs/event-schema.md`, `docs/cli-reference.md`; Acceptance Criteria (all five ACs)
scenarios: An implementer ships the code changes, all ACs pass green, but the doc files are never updated — the plan provides no gate to catch this.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC6: '`docs/ticket-model.md`, `docs/event-schema.md`, and `docs/cli-reference.md` each mention `caused_by` and describe its semantics — verified by grep or a docs-lint step in CI.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the `_hierarchy.py` plan note: `caused_by` is automatically non-blocking because `_hierarchy.py:170` gates on `_BLOCKING_RELATIONS = frozenset({'blocks','depends_on'})` — no edit to `_hierarchy.py` is needed; replace 'verify the non-blocking relation set' with a confirmation that no change is required.
evidence: _hierarchy.py line 170: `if relation not in _BLOCKING_RELATIONS:` (returns early as non-blocking)
_relations.py line 12: `_BLOCKING_RELATIONS = frozenset({'blocks', 'depends_on'})` — only two members, no enumeration of non-blocking relations
No search hit for an explicit non-blocking relation frozenset/tuple in _hierarchy.py
Plan quote: 'verify the non-blocking relation set includes caused_by (promotion applies only to blocks/depends_on)'
findings.finding: The plan asserts `_hierarchy.py` needs to be edited to 'verify the non-blocking relation set includes `caused_by`', implying an explicit enumeration exists there. In reality, `_hierarchy.py` line 170 uses `_BLOCKING_RELATIONS` (a frozenset of only `{blocks, depends_on}` from `_relations.py`) to classify non-blocking relations — there is no explicit non-blocking relation set to update. `caused_by` is automatically non-blocking without any change to `_hierarchy.py`. The plan's premise is unverifiable and risks misdirecting a developer to add an incorrect explicit enumeration.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/graph/_hierarchy.py` — 'verify the non-blocking relation set includes `caused_by`'
scenarios: Developer reads the plan, searches _hierarchy.py for an explicit non-blocking set, finds none, and either (a) introduces a new hardcoded tuple that duplicates/conflicts with the _BLOCKING_RELATIONS pattern, or (b) wastes time wondering what to verify.
A code reviewer approves a change to _hierarchy.py that adds a redundant explicit enumeration, creating a dual-maintenance liability.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove `_hierarchy.py` from the Files edit list or change the note to: '`_hierarchy.py` — NO CHANGE NEEDED: promotion is gated on `_BLOCKING_RELATIONS` (only `blocks`/`depends_on`), so `caused_by` is automatically non-blocking without code modification.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the conditional 'if that set is explicit' bullet with a committed edit specification: either name the constant/set and the exact line to update, or state 'no explicit set exists — Jira-unmapping is handled by the absence of a mapping entry, no edit needed'.
evidence: 'add it to the unmapped set if that set is explicit'
The 'if' clause requires the executor to first investigate whether an explicit unmapped set exists before knowing what edit to make
findings.finding: The `acli_graph.py` / `outbound_links.py` bullet says 'add it to the unmapped set if that set is explicit' — a conditional that defers a real design decision to the executor (investigate the code structure, then decide what to do).
scenarios: Executor finds no explicit unmapped set and, with no guidance, either skips the file (risking a Jira-sync bug) or adds unnecessary boilerplate.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion asserting that the MCP write surface accepts `caused_by` (e.g. a unit test that invokes the MCP link handler with `caused_by` and asserts it does not raise a validation error).
evidence: Files section: 'src/rebar/_mcp_writes.py — update the relation list in the MCP write surface'
AC1–AC5 test CLI round-trip, schema membership, graph semantics, golden help, and back-compat — none assert MCP surface correctness
findings.finding: `src/rebar/_mcp_writes.py` is listed as a Files deliverable (update the relation list in the MCP write surface) but no acceptance criterion covers this change, leaving it without a verifiable gate.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/_mcp_writes.py`; Acceptance Criteria (all five ACs)
scenarios: The MCP write surface still rejects `caused_by` at runtime because `_mcp_writes.py` was never updated, but all ACs pass green.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly document what happens to already-persisted `caused_by` events if the feature is rolled back, and confirm whether the reducer/reader must be tolerant of unknown relation values (or specify a store-cleanup step).
evidence: Approach: 'back-out is simply ceasing to emit the value'
No mention of store cleanup, migration, or how old-code validators handle a `caused_by` value they don't recognise in their enum
findings.finding: The rollback/back-out path states 'back-out is simply ceasing to emit the value' but does not address (a) already-persisted `caused_by` link events that remain in the store after rollback, or (b) old-code consumers reading new events that contain `caused_by` — leaving the reversibility story incomplete under T4's requirement for an explicit rollback mechanism.
findings.location: Approach section — 'back-out is simply ceasing to emit the value'
scenarios: A rollback is initiated after `caused_by` links have been written to persistent storage; old code re-reading those events either crashes on schema validation or silently mis-classifies them.
A downstream consumer with a strict `Relation` literal type reads a stored event containing `caused_by` after the feature is rolled back — the literal no longer includes the value.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Approach: 'If rolled back, any persisted `caused_by` link events are inert (the reducer ignores unknown relation values) — confirm the reducer has an explicit `else: continue` / passthrough for unrecognised relation strings, or note that a store sweep is required.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (and document in the plan) whether `acli_graph.py`/`outbound_links.py` have an explicit unmapped-relations set; if yes, unconditionally add `caused_by` to it and add an AC asserting the reconciler does not attempt to sync `caused_by` edges to Jira.
evidence: Plan states: 'add it to the unmapped set if that set is explicit' — the conditionality is unresolved; the plan does not confirm whether the set is or is not explicit
No AC or test validates that `caused_by` is NOT sent to Jira / does not cause a reconciler error
The `discovered_from` precedent is cited but no test verifies `caused_by` follows the same Jira-unmapped path
findings.finding: The Jira-unmapped consumer update is specified conditionally ('add it to the unmapped set if that set is explicit'), leaving open the case where the set IS explicit and `caused_by` is not added. In that scenario the Jira reconciler would attempt to sync the relation to Jira (no mapping exists), potentially erroring on every ticket with a `caused_by` edge or silently dropping it, with no AC or test to catch either outcome.
findings.location: `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/acli_graph.py`, `outbound_links.py` — 'add it to the unmapped set if that set is explicit'
scenarios: Jira reconciler processes a ticket with a `caused_by` link, finds no mapping, throws an uncaught exception, and halts reconciliation for that ticket or batch
Reconciler silently skips the unmapped relation but logs nothing, making the omission invisible to operators
findings.suggested_fix: Grep for the `discovered_from` entry in both files; wherever it appears in an explicit unmapped/exclusion set, add `caused_by` beside it, and add a unit test that mocks the Jira client and asserts no call is made for a `caused_by` link event.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the 'verify' bullet for `_hierarchy.py` with a committed decision: either 'no edit needed — `_hierarchy.py` does not maintain an explicit relation set' OR 'add `caused_by` to the `NON_BLOCKING_RELATIONS` constant at line ~N'.
evidence: 'verify the non-blocking relation set includes caused_by (promotion applies only to blocks/depends_on)'
The word 'verify' signals an investigation step, not a committed edit — the executor must inspect the file first and decide
findings.finding: The `_hierarchy.py` file-edit bullet says 'verify the non-blocking relation set includes `caused_by`' — a 'verify whether' placeholder that defers the real decision (does this file need editing at all?) to the executor rather than committing to a concrete change.
scenarios: Executor reads the bullet, opens the file, finds no explicit set to update, and is left guessing whether to add one or skip the file entirely.
findings.suggested_fix: State the outcome explicitly, e.g.: '`src/rebar/graph/_hierarchy.py` — no edit needed; promotion logic guards on `blocks`/`depends_on` by name and `caused_by` is automatically excluded. If an explicit set exists, add `caused_by` there (line ~N).' This gives the executor a default action and a fallback.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and corresponding unit test) asserting that a `caused_by` link is NOT included in the Jira outbound sync payload — e.g., assert the unmapped-relations set in `acli_graph.py`/`outbound_links.py` contains `caused_by` and that no Jira link-update call is made for a `caused_by` edge.
evidence: Plan says: 'leave `caused_by` intentionally Jira-unmapped (like `discovered_from`/`duplicates`/`supersedes`); add it to the unmapped set if that set is explicit'
No AC asserts that `caused_by` is absent from Jira outbound link payloads
The five ACs cover round-trip, schema membership, non-blocking semantics, golden snapshots, and back-compat — none covers Jira-unmapping
Changed behavior: new relation value that must be excluded from an existing sync path, with no corresponding test
findings.finding: The test plan includes no assertion that `caused_by` is absent from (i.e., not forwarded to) Jira sync. The plan identifies an explicit unmapped set in `acli_graph.py`/`outbound_links.py` and says to add `caused_by` to it 'if that set is explicit' — meaning this is a conditionally required code edit. No AC or unit test verifies that a `caused_by` link is NOT emitted to the Jira sync path, so a missed or incorrect edit to the unmapped set would be undetected by the test suite.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/acli_graph.py`, `outbound_links.py`; Tests section; AC list
scenarios: If `acli_graph.py` has an explicit whitelist/blacklist of unmapped relations and the edit is omitted or typo'd, `caused_by` edges would be forwarded to Jira on every reconcile, silently creating spurious Jira links — and the test suite would remain green.
findings.suggested_fix: In `tests/unit/graph/test_caused_by_relation.py`, add a test that inspects the explicit unmapped-relations set (e.g., `assert 'caused_by' in JIRA_UNMAPPED_RELATIONS`) and/or patches the Jira write surface and asserts it is not called when reconciling a ticket with only `caused_by` links.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's 'single behavioral gotcha' framing: there is a SECOND hardcoded relation enumeration in `_links.py` at the per-level cycle guard comment (line 225); add updating that comment to the `_links.py` edit spec.
evidence: _links.py lines 224-228: `# Only the cycle-capable relations (blocks / depends_on) are subject to the per-level cycle guard; relates_to / duplicates / supersedes / discovered_from are non-blocking...` followed by `if relation in ('blocks', 'depends_on')`
_graph.py line 204: the tuple the plan calls the 'single behavioral gotcha'
Plan quote: 'the single behavioral gotcha is _graph.py's hardcoded non-blocking tuple, which must list caused_by or the relation wrongly gets blocking-cycle semantics'
Plan Files section for _links.py only says 'add `caused_by` to `CANONICAL_RELATIONS`' — no mention of the stale per-level comment
findings.finding: The plan calls `_graph.py` line ~204 the 'single behavioral gotcha' and the only hardcoded tuple that needs `caused_by`. However, `_links.py` lines 224-228 contain a SECOND hardcoded tuple `if relation in ('blocks', 'depends_on')` for the per-level cycle guard, with a comment at line 225 that explicitly enumerates 'relates_to / duplicates / supersedes / discovered_from' as non-cycle-inducing — this comment becomes stale when `caused_by` is added. While the code behavior is correct (caused_by naturally falls through), the plan's 'single behavioral gotcha' framing is a false premise that could cause a developer to miss updating the stale comment.
findings.location: Approach section: 'the single behavioral gotcha is `_graph.py`'s hardcoded non-blocking tuple'; Files section: `src/rebar/graph/_links.py`
scenarios: Developer updates _graph.py:204 tuple correctly, adds caused_by to CANONICAL_RELATIONS, but misses the stale comment at _links.py:225 that still lists only the old 6 relations — leaving confusing documentation claiming caused_by has blocking-cycle semantics.
A future developer reading _links.py:225 concludes caused_by was intentionally omitted from the non-cycle list.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Files section for `_links.py`, add: 'also update the comment at line 225 to include `caused_by` in the list of non-cycle-inducing relations.' In the Approach section, change 'single behavioral gotcha' to 'primary behavioral gotcha' and note the secondary stale comment.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the `acli_graph.py` and `outbound_links.py` edit spec: update the inline comments enumerating unmapped relations (lines ~40-41 in each file) to include `caused_by` alongside `duplicates / supersedes / discovered_from`.
evidence: outbound_links.py line 40-41: `# Relations with no reliable Jira link type (duplicates / supersedes / discovered_from) are intentionally ABSENT and SKIPPED by the differ.`
acli_graph.py line 41: `# Relations with no reliable Jira link type (``duplicates`` / ``supersedes`` / ``discovered_from``) are intentionally ABSENT`
Plan quote: 'leave `caused_by` intentionally Jira-unmapped (like `discovered_from`/`duplicates`/`supersedes`); add it to the unmapped set if that set is explicit'
findings.finding: The plan instructs leaving `caused_by` 'intentionally Jira-unmapped (like `discovered_from`/`duplicates`/`supersedes`); add it to the unmapped set if that set is explicit.' Both `outbound_links.py` (line 40-41) and `acli_graph.py` (line 41) contain inline comments that enumerate the currently-unmapped relations as `duplicates / supersedes / discovered_from`. These comments are not guarded by the conditionality ('if that set is explicit') — they are human-readable documentation that will become stale when `caused_by` is added, and the plan makes no mention of updating them.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/acli_graph.py`, `outbound_links.py` — 'add it to the unmapped set if that set is explicit'
scenarios: Both reconciler files' comments still enumerate only 3 unmapped relations after the change, causing confusion about whether `caused_by` is intentionally unmapped or accidentally omitted from Jira sync.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Files section for `acli_graph.py` and `outbound_links.py`, add: 'update the comment listing unmapped relations to include `caused_by`' alongside the existing conditional 'add it to the unmapped set if that set is explicit'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `caused_by` to `_UNMAPPED_RELATIONS` in `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/diffing/test_link_dedup.py` so the parametrized skip test covers the new relation.
evidence: _UNMAPPED_RELATIONS = ["duplicates", "supersedes", "discovered_from"] at line 123
@pytest.mark.parametrize("relation", _UNMAPPED_RELATIONS) at line 150
Plan Files section does not list this test file as an edit target
Plan says 'add it to the unmapped set if that set is explicit' — this IS an explicit set in a test file
findings.finding: tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/diffing/test_link_dedup.py contains a hardcoded _UNMAPPED_RELATIONS = ["duplicates", "supersedes", "discovered_from"] list that parametrizes test_outbound_diff_links_skips_unmapped_relations, which asserts each relation is skipped by the outbound differ. This file is not in the plan's edit set, so after caused_by is added to the system it will not be covered by that parametrized test — leaving a gap in the Jira-unmapped skip assertion.
scenarios: caused_by is added to CANONICAL_RELATIONS but not to _UNMAPPED_RELATIONS in test_link_dedup.py — the 'skips unmapped relations' parametrized test never runs against caused_by, so a future regression that accidentally maps caused_by to a Jira type would not be caught.
findings.suggested_fix: _UNMAPPED_RELATIONS = ["duplicates", "supersedes", "discovered_from", "caused_by"]

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `"caused_by"` to the `@pytest.mark.parametrize` list in `tests/scripts/graph/test_graph_hierarchy_resolve.py:240` (`test_resolve_hierarchy_link_non_blocking_never_promoted`) so hierarchy non-promotion is asserted for the new relation.
evidence: @pytest.mark.parametrize("relation", ["relates_to", "duplicates", "supersedes", "discovered_from"]) at line 240
test_resolve_hierarchy_link_non_blocking_never_promoted asserts was_redirected=False for each relation
Plan Files section does not list this file
findings.finding: tests/scripts/graph/test_graph_hierarchy_resolve.py:240 parametrizes over ["relates_to", "duplicates", "supersedes", "discovered_from"] to assert non-blocking relations are never promoted by the hierarchy resolver. caused_by follows the same non-blocking/no-promotion semantics but is not in this list and this file is not in the plan's edit set.
scenarios: If a future _hierarchy.py change accidentally adds caused_by to the blocking path, this parametrized hierarchy-promotion test would not catch it because caused_by is not in the parametrize list.
findings.suggested_fix: @pytest.mark.parametrize("relation", ["relates_to", "duplicates", "supersedes", "discovered_from", "caused_by"])

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `"caused_by"` to the `@pytest.mark.parametrize` list in `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_session_log_type.py:69` (`test_nonblocking_links_allowed`) so the session_log non-blocking permission is asserted for the new relation.
evidence: @pytest.mark.parametrize("relation", ["relates_to", "discovered_from"]) at line 69
test_nonblocking_links_allowed asserts rebar.link(..., relation) does not raise for a session_log endpoint
Plan does not list this file as an edit target
_hierarchy.py non-blocking branch (line 170) uses _BLOCKING_RELATIONS membership — caused_by will pass through as non-blocking once added to CANONICAL_RELATIONS
findings.finding: tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_session_log_type.py:69 parametrizes ["relates_to", "discovered_from"] to assert non-blocking relations are allowed on session_log endpoints. caused_by is non-blocking and should also be permitted on session_log endpoints (per _hierarchy.py's non-blocking branch), but this file is not in the plan's edit set and caused_by is not in the parametrize list.
scenarios: If caused_by is accidentally rejected for session_log endpoints (e.g. a guard is added without updating the non-blocking check), this gap means no existing test would catch the regression.
findings.suggested_fix: @pytest.mark.parametrize("relation", ["relates_to", "discovered_from", "caused_by"])

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] When updating `src/rebar/_mcp_writes.py` relation list, also change 'one of the **six** canonical relations' → 'one of the **seven** canonical relations' in the `link_tickets` docstring.
evidence: """Link two tickets (one of the six canonical relations: blocks | depends_on | relates_to | duplicates | supersedes | discovered_from).""" at line 194
Plan says 'update the relation list' — the word 'six' is a separate string that could be missed
findings.finding: The _mcp_writes.py docstring at line 194 reads 'one of the **six** canonical relations' — this count will be stale after caused_by is added. The plan says to 'update the relation list in the MCP write surface' but does not explicitly call out the count string, creating a risk the prose count is missed during editing.
findings.location: src/rebar/_mcp_writes.py:194; plan Files section ('update the relation list in the MCP write surface')
scenarios: A developer updates the pipe-separated relation list in the docstring but misses the count word 'six', leaving stale documentation visible to MCP consumers and LLMs reading the tool schema.
findings.suggested_fix: """Link two tickets (one of the seven canonical relations: blocks | depends_on | relates_to | duplicates | supersedes | discovered_from | caused_by)."""

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_lib_writes.py` to the Files list and update the `link()` docstring at line ~593 from 'six canonical relations' to 'seven canonical relations' with `caused_by` included in the enumeration.
evidence: src/rebar/_lib_writes.py line 593: '``relation`` must be one of the six canonical relations: blocks, depends_on, relates_to, duplicates, supersedes, discovered_from.'
The plan explicitly updates _mcp_writes.py link_tickets docstring ('one of the six canonical relations') but does not mention the identical language in _lib_writes.py
findings.finding: The plan's Files list omits src/rebar/_lib_writes.py, which contains a docstring for the public link() facade (line 593) that hardcodes 'the six canonical relations: blocks, depends_on, relates_to, duplicates, supersedes, discovered_from' — this will be stale after adding caused_by as the 7th relation.
findings.location: Files section (absence of src/rebar/_lib_writes.py); src/rebar/_lib_writes.py line 593
scenarios: After the change, a developer reading the public Python facade's docstring sees 'six canonical relations' and an enumeration that omits caused_by, causing confusion or leading them to believe caused_by is not accessible via the Python library surface
findings.suggested_fix: Change the docstring to: '``relation`` must be one of the seven canonical relations: blocks, depends_on, relates_to, duplicates, supersedes, discovered_from, caused_by.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a post-rollback assertion: specify whether the reducer must tolerate (ignore/warn) unknown relation values after rollback so that previously persisted `caused_by` events do not cause errors or silent data loss, and document the store state after rollback.
evidence: Plan quote: 'back-out is simply ceasing to emit the value'
AC item 5 asserts the reducer handles 'a fixture of old links' but 'old' means pre-`caused_by`, not post-rollback `caused_by` events
Post-rollback schema state for already-persisted `caused_by` events is not addressed
T11 rubric: 'the plan must assert on the schema STATE AFTER rollback, not merely that rollback exits 0'
findings.finding: The plan states rollback is 'simply ceasing to emit the value', but does not assert the schema/reducer state AFTER rollback when `caused_by` LINK events have already been persisted. If the enum is removed from the schema and types, the reducer may reject or silently drop previously written `caused_by` events, and the plan is silent on whether those events are lost, ignored, or cause a hard error.
findings.location: Approach section — 'back-out is simply ceasing to emit the value'; AC item 5 (back-compat)
scenarios: Team links several bugs with `caused_by`, then rolls back the feature by removing the enum value; the store still contains `caused_by` events; on next reduce/query the reducer encounters an unrecognized relation and either errors or silently drops those links — provenance data is lost.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a reducer invariant: 'unknown relation values in persisted LINK events are logged and skipped (not hard-errored), so a rollback of the caused_by enum leaves all other link data intact; caused_by edges become invisible but are not deleted from the event log.' Add a unit test asserting the reducer handles an unknown relation gracefully (distinct from AC item 5's old-relation fixture).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_lib_writes.py` (the `link()` docstring line ~592-593) to the Files section and update the enumerated relation list from six to seven canonical relations, adding `caused_by`.
evidence: Confirmed by search: 'relation`` must be one of the six canonical relations: blocks, depends_on, relates_to, duplicates, supersedes, discovered_from.'
ABSENCE: `_lib_writes.py` does not appear in the plan's Files list or Approach section.
The plan's Files section lists `_mcp_writes.py` for the 'MCP write surface' update but omits the parallel Python-facade docstring.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/_lib_writes.py` contains a public API docstring (line ~592-593) that enumerates 'the six canonical relations: blocks, depends_on, relates_to, duplicates, supersedes, discovered_from' — an explicit, enumerated vocabulary that will be stale after adding `caused_by`. This file is not listed in the plan's Files section.
findings.location: src/rebar/_lib_writes.py line ~592-593 (link() docstring); Plan section: Files
scenarios: An agent/LLM consuming the `rebar.link()` docstring as its interface spec will not know `caused_by` is valid and may refuse to emit it.
A static-analysis or doc-generation tool enumerating relation values from docstrings will produce a stale 6-item list.
findings.suggested_fix: Update the `link()` docstring: ``relation`` must be one of the seven canonical relations: blocks, depends_on, relates_to, duplicates, supersedes, discovered_from, caused_by.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/reducer/_managed_refs.py` (lines ~47-49) to the Files section and update its comment to include `caused_by` in the enumerated list of unmapped/unsynced relations.
evidence: Confirmed by file read: 'Relations with no reliable peer link type (duplicates / supersedes / discovered_from) are intentionally absent — they are never synced, so they are never "managed" for removal-propagation purposes.'
ABSENCE: `_managed_refs.py` is not listed in the plan's Files section.
Plan explicitly calls out `acli_graph.py` and `outbound_links.py` for the same unmapped-set documentation, but misses this third parallel site.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/reducer/_managed_refs.py` contains an explicit comment (lines ~47-49) enumerating the unmapped/unsynced relations ('duplicates / supersedes / discovered_from are intentionally absent') that must include `caused_by` — but this file is not mentioned in the plan's Files section despite the plan calling out `acli_graph.py` and `outbound_links.py` for the identical purpose.
findings.location: src/rebar/reducer/_managed_refs.py lines ~47-49; Plan section: Files (`acli_graph.py`, `outbound_links.py` entry)
scenarios: A developer or LLM reading `_managed_refs.py`'s MANAGED_REF_KINDS comment as the authoritative unmapped-set definition will not know `caused_by` is also unmapped, and could mistakenly attempt to sync it outbound to Jira.
findings.suggested_fix: Amend the comment: 'Relations with no reliable peer link type (duplicates / supersedes / discovered_from / caused_by) are intentionally absent.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the `src/rebar/graph/_links.py` Files entry to also update the inline comment at line ~225 in `add_dependency` to include `caused_by` in the enumerated non-blocking relation list.
evidence: Confirmed by file read: '# Only the cycle-capable relations (blocks / depends_on) are subject to the\n# per-level cycle guard; relates_to / duplicates / supersedes / discovered_from\n# are non-blocking and never cycle-inducing (mirrors check_would_create_cycle).'
Plan only says: 'add `caused_by` to `CANONICAL_RELATIONS`' for this file — does not mention the co-located comment at line 225.
Instruction-locality gap: the comment and the enforcement set it explains are in the same function body but the plan specifies only the frozenset change.
findings.finding: The inline comment in `src/rebar/graph/_links.py` at line ~225 enumerates the non-blocking, non-cycle-inducing relation set ('relates_to / duplicates / supersedes / discovered_from') without `caused_by`. The plan lists updating `CANONICAL_RELATIONS` in this file but does not specify updating this co-located comment, which is the only in-code human-readable explanation of the semantics.
findings.location: src/rebar/graph/_links.py line ~225 (inline comment in `add_dependency`); Plan section: Files (`src/rebar/graph/_links.py` entry)
scenarios: A reviewer or LLM reading the `add_dependency` function body sees a comment listing the non-blocking relations WITHOUT `caused_by`, contradicting the frozenset above — creating confusion about whether the omission is intentional.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the comment to: '# per-level cycle guard; relates_to / duplicates / supersedes / discovered_from / caused_by\n# are non-blocking and never cycle-inducing (mirrors check_would_create_cycle).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consolidate the non-blocking relation set into a single authoritative constant (e.g., `NON_BLOCKING_RELATIONS` in `_links.py`) and reference it from both `_graph.py` and `_hierarchy.py`, so future relation additions have one edit site instead of three.
evidence: "CRITICAL: line ~204 has a hardcoded tuple ('relates_to','duplicates','supersedes','discovered_from') that returns False (non-blocking / non-cycle); caused_by MUST be added there or it falls to the else-branch and gets block-cycle semantics."
"verify the non-blocking relation set includes caused_by (promotion applies only to blocks/depends_on)"
Plan perpetuates multi-site hardcoded enumeration without acknowledging consolidation as a follow-up or alternative.
findings.finding: The non-blocking/non-cycle relation classification is hardcoded in at least three separate locations (_graph.py hardcoded tuple, _hierarchy.py non-blocking set, _links.py CANONICAL_RELATIONS), and the plan adds caused_by to each independently without proposing consolidation. This means every future relation addition requires touching all sites or risks wrong blocking semantics — the plan's own 'CRITICAL' callout evidences this fragility.
scenarios: A future 8th relation is added, the author updates _links.py and the schema but misses _graph.py's hardcoded tuple, silently giving the new relation blocking-cycle semantics — exactly the bug this plan's CRITICAL note warns about.
A reviewer auditing graph semantics must cross-reference three files to determine which relations are non-blocking, with no single authoritative source.
findings.suggested_fix: In `_links.py`, define `NON_BLOCKING_RELATIONS: frozenset[str] = frozenset({"relates_to", "duplicates", "supersedes", "discovered_from", "caused_by"})`. In `_graph.py` replace the hardcoded tuple with `from rebar.graph._links import NON_BLOCKING_RELATIONS` and check `relation in NON_BLOCKING_RELATIONS`. Do the same in `_hierarchy.py`. This makes the non-blocking classification a single-source constant.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC and unit-test scenario that `rebar unlink <a> <b> caused_by` succeeds (exits 0) and the edge no longer appears in `rebar show <a> --output json` after the unlink.
evidence: AC only covers `rebar link <a> <b> caused_by` exits 0 and appears in show output; no AC for `rebar unlink <a> <b> caused_by`
Plan says update unlink.py 'the directional-relations note (line ~30)' — implies directional relations may be specially handled, raising the question of whether unlink validates directionality
No unit test scenario for the unlink path is described in the Tests section
findings.finding: The plan updates `unlink.py` help text to include `caused_by` but no Acceptance Criterion or unit test covers the unlink round-trip (`rebar unlink <a> <b> caused_by` after a link). If `unlink.py` validates the relation against an allowlist or the command otherwise rejects unknown relations, the new value might silently fail to unlink, leaving a dangling edge the user cannot remove.
scenarios: User creates a `caused_by` link and later wants to remove it; if unlink rejects the relation string, the edge is permanent until direct store manipulation
CI passes all described tests but an unlink bug ships to users
findings.suggested_fix: In `test_caused_by_relation.py`, after the link round-trip test, add: call `rebar unlink a b caused_by`, assert exit 0, then assert `rebar show a --output json | jq '.links[]|select(.relation=="caused_by")'` returns empty.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether Jira's standard link vocabulary includes a 'Causes'/'is caused by' type and document the explicit decision (map or intentionally leave unmapped with rationale) in the plan before implementation.
evidence: Plan states: 'leave `caused_by` intentionally Jira-unmapped (like `discovered_from`/`duplicates`/`supersedes`)' without checking Jira's native link vocabulary.

Jira's built-in link type vocabulary includes 'Causes' / 'is caused by' in many Jira Software configurations (a standard issue link type distinct from 'Blocks' and 'Relates'), meaning a mapping may already exist and the Jira-unmapped decision may silently drop data for Jira-synced teams.
The `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` dict in both `outbound_links.py` and `acli_graph.py` is the only gating artifact — adding a `caused_by` entry there would be a one-liner if a Jira type exists.
ABSENCE: the plan provides no evidence that Jira's 'Causes' link type was checked or ruled out.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `caused_by` should be Jira-unmapped (like `discovered_from`) without verifying whether Jira natively supports a 'Causes'/'is caused by' link type that could provide a mapping — omitting prior-art consideration of how the industry-standard Jira tracker models causal bug provenance.
scenarios: A team using rebar+Jira sync creates `bug_A caused_by change_B` links. Because `caused_by` is Jira-unmapped, these edges are silently dropped from the Jira view, making the 'source of bugs' traversal invisible in Jira even when a native Jira link type exists for it.

If a Jira instance has 'Causes'/'is caused by' configured, a later decision to map `caused_by` becomes a migration requiring replay of all existing `caused_by` LINK events against Jira — avoidable if the mapping is decided upfront.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to the plan's Jira-unmapped rationale citing whether Jira's 'Causes' link type was checked. If a mapping is viable, add `'caused_by': ('Causes', False)` to `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` in both reconciler files alongside the `_UNMAPPED_RELATIONS` test list update. If intentionally unmapped, document the explicit tradeoff (e.g., 'Jira's Causes type exists but is not universally configured; leaving unmapped avoids coupling to a non-standard Jira setup').

findings.finding: Multiple golden test fixture files in tests/golden/cli_help/ hardcode the 6-relation string (e.g. 'blocks|depends_on|relates_to|duplicates|supersedes|discovered_from') verbatim. These fixtures are asserted byte-for-byte by golden tests. Adding caused_by to link.txt (named in the plan) will update the help output but the golden files will diverge, causing golden test failures. The plan's Files list does not include these fixtures.

src/rebar/_commands/unlink.py:28 hardcodes the relation list in its help text ('relation: blocks | depends_on | relates_to | duplicates | supersedes | discovered_from') and is not named in the plan's Files list. After adding caused_by, unlink's help string will be stale, inconsistent with link's help.

src/rebar/_commands/unlink.py is not named in the plan's Files section but hardcodes the 6-relation list in its _USAGE string (lines 28 and 30), which will display stale help text after caused_by is added.
src/rebar/_cli/help/overview.txt is not named in the plan's Files section but hardcodes the 6-relation list on line 16 for the `link` subcommand description.

Eight golden test snapshot files under tests/golden/cli_help/ (e.g. link__help.out, link__dashhelp.out, __overview_help.out, __overview_dashhelp.out, __overview_noargs.out, __overview_dashh.out, __help_unknown__.err, __unknown__.out) hardcode the 6-relation help text and will fail as soon as link.txt or overview.txt is updated, but these files are not listed in the plan.

Several hardcoded relation-list strings that enumerate the full relation vocabulary are not covered by the plan's Files list: `src/rebar/_commands/unlink.py` (usage string, line 28-30) and `src/rebar/_cli/help/overview.txt` (line 16). These will display a stale 6-value relation list to users after `caused_by` is added, creating a user-visible documentation inconsistency.

`src/rebar/graph/_graph.py` is missing from the Files list, but `check_would_create_cycle` has a hardcoded allowlist of non-cycle relations (`if relation in ("relates_to", "duplicates", "supersedes", "discovered_from"): return False`). Without adding `caused_by` to this list, a `caused_by` link will fall through to the `else` branch (blocks semantics), run `_get_all_blocked_by(target_id)`, and potentially raise a spurious `CyclicDependencyError` for any `caused_by` pair that shares a path with blocking edges — contradicting the plan's stated 'non-cycle-inducing' property.
findings.suggested_fix: In `_graph.py`, change `if relation in ("relates_to", "duplicates", "supersedes", "discovered_from"):` to `if relation in ("relates_to", "duplicates", "supersedes", "discovered_from", "caused_by"):` and add `src/rebar/graph/_graph.py` to the Files list in the plan.

findings.finding: `src/rebar/graph/_graph.py` (line 204) contains a hardcoded inline tuple of non-blocking relations — `('relates_to', 'duplicates', 'supersedes', 'discovered_from')` — rather than deriving the set from `CANONICAL_RELATIONS - _BLOCKING_RELATIONS`. This is a pre-existing NIH micro-pattern (an inline copy that can diverge from the authoritative frozenset). The plan omits `_graph.py` from the Files section, so when `caused_by` is added to `CANONICAL_RELATIONS` only, `check_would_create_cycle` will NOT treat `caused_by` as non-cycle-inducing: the else-branch will run cycle detection on it, contradicting the plan's stated 'non-cycle like discovered_from' property.
findings.suggested_fix: In `_graph.py:204`, change `if relation in ('relates_to', 'duplicates', 'supersedes', 'discovered_from'):` to `if relation not in _BLOCKING_RELATIONS:` (importing `_BLOCKING_RELATIONS` from `._relations`), making the check self-maintaining as new non-blocking relations are added.

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'non-cycle like discovered_from' but _graph.py:204 has a HARDCODED tuple — not driven by CANONICAL_RELATIONS — that explicitly exempts named relations from cycle detection. `caused_by` is absent from this tuple, so check_would_create_cycle will fall through to the blocking-relation branch and incorrectly treat caused_by as cycle-capable. The plan names only _links.py under Files; _graph.py is omitted.
findings.suggested_fix: Change _graph.py:204 from `if relation in ("relates_to", "duplicates", "supersedes", "discovered_from"):` to `if relation in ("relates_to", "duplicates", "supersedes", "discovered_from", "caused_by"):`

findings.finding: src/rebar/graph/_graph.py line 204 contains an explicit allowlist `if relation in ('relates_to', 'duplicates', 'supersedes', 'discovered_from'): return False` in `check_would_create_cycle`. Without adding `caused_by` to this allowlist, a `caused_by` link would fall through to the `blocks` cycle-check branch, incorrectly applying a cycle guard to a non-blocking relation.
findings.suggested_fix: Change `if relation in ("relates_to", "duplicates", "supersedes", "discovered_from"):` to `if relation in ("relates_to", "duplicates", "supersedes", "discovered_from", "caused_by"):`

findings.finding: `_graph.py:check_would_create_cycle` contains a hardcoded inline tuple `('relates_to', 'duplicates', 'supersedes', 'discovered_from')` at line 204 that governs which relations bypass cycle-detection. The plan lists files to update but omits `_graph.py`, so `caused_by` will NOT be in this allowlist after the change. Consequently `check_would_create_cycle('a','a','caused_by',...)` will NOT short-circuit to `False` and instead runs the `blocks`-path BFS, causing the plan's own AC ('a `caused_by` self-cycle does not raise') to FAIL.
findings.suggested_fix: In `src/rebar/graph/_graph.py` line 204, change `if relation in ('relates_to', 'duplicates', 'supersedes', 'discovered_from'):` to `if relation in ('relates_to', 'duplicates', 'supersedes', 'discovered_from', 'caused_by'):`. Alternatively, derive this set from `CANONICAL_RELATIONS - _BLOCKING_RELATIONS` to make future additions automatically correct.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
